theme: The office breathes — now teach it to remember, adapt, and surprise
visual_observations: Desktop (1280x800): After-hours vibe active. All #49 improvements confirmed live — door shows 🔒 Locked, candle flickers on manager desk, window displays 'golden hour' caption, whiteboard has history dots, Bloberto speaks on mount ~3s after load. After-hours quote pool now has 8 entries (doubled). The office is atmospherically rich but interaction-poor on mobile — characters overlap below 480px, conference table clips. prefers-reduced-motion only applies to desk-away-sign. The idle wander paths feel samey after 2 minutes. No keyboard focus indicators on office-floor characters despite tabIndex={0}.

ideas.title: Respect prefers-reduced-motion Globally
ideas.description: Office.css only has ONE @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) rule (for desk-away-sign). But the office has 20+ animations: manager-wobble, bob, enter-office, ghost-float, idle-wander, lamp-pulse, candle-flicker, coffee-wobble, typing-dots, ambient-particle, heat-shimmer, vibe-shockwave, door-swing, clock-frantic-tick, etc. Users who enable reduced motion preferences get ALL of these. Fix: add a global rule at the top of Office.css: @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { .char, .char--manager, .char--working, .char--idle, .char--ghost, .desk-lamp, .mgr-desk__candle, .mgr-desk__coffee, .mgr-desk__trophy, .coffee-steam span, .typing-dots span, .ambient-particle, .office-door, .vibe-shockwave, .clock-minute-hand--frantic, .idle-micro-bubble, .confetti-piece { animation: none !important; transition: none !important; } }. WHY: This is an accessibility requirement (WCAG 2.1 SC 2.3.3). The office is a vestibular trigger nightmare with its current animation density. One CSS block fixes it for all current and future animations. Zero JS changes needed.

ideas.title: Focus Ring on Office Characters
ideas.description: Every Character component has tabIndex={0} (Office.jsx Character component) and role='button' when clickable, but there's only one CSS rule for focus: .char:focus-visible .char__avatar { outline: 2px solid var(--accent); outline-offset: 2px; }. However, the .char itself has pointer-events: none as the base state (Office.css .char rule), which means keyboard focus technically works but the visual ring is invisible behind the pointer-events: none layer. Fix: ensure .char:focus-visible has pointer-events: auto and add a subtle glow: .char:focus-visible { pointer-events: auto; } .char:focus-visible .char__avatar { outline: 2px solid var(--accent); outline-offset: 3px; box-shadow: 0 0 12px var(--accent-glow); }. WHY: Keyboard users tabbing through the office can't see where they are. The office view is the hero feature — making it keyboard-navigable completes the accessibility story started by the dashboard's proper aria roles.

ideas.title: Idle Workers Get Unique Wander Personalities
ideas.description: Currently idle workers all use the same 4 wander patterns (char--wander-1 through char--wander-4) and the same IDLE_BUBBLE_EMOJIS map ({1:'☕', 2:'💬', 3:'🪟', 4:'🧘'}). The wander CSS paths are fixed sine-wave translations. When 3+ workers are idle, they all drift in the same rhythm. Fix: in Office.css, give each wander variant a different animation-duration and path shape. wander-1: 12s, circular. wander-2: 16s, figure-8. wander-3: 20s, diagonal drift. wander-4: 14s, small circle near desk. Also: tie IDLE_BUBBLE_EMOJIS to the worker's role instead of wander index — Carlos (Backend) gets 🔧, Maya (Frontend) gets 🎨, Dave (DevOps) gets 🔥, Sofia (QA) gets 🔍. WHY: Idle characters are on screen longest (they're not doing tasks). If they all wander identically, the illusion breaks. Unique movement + role-aware thought bubbles make each character feel like a person, not a sprite clone.

ideas.title: Conference Table Shows Meeting Timer
ideas.description: ConferenceTable (Office.jsx) shows a 'Huddle' badge when 2+ idle workers are present, but there's no sense of duration. Fix: track when the meeting started by storing a ref in the Office component: const meetingStartRef = useRef(null). When meetingWorkers goes from 0 to 2+, set meetingStartRef.current = Date.now(). When it drops to <2, clear it. Pass the start time to ConferenceTable. Display a small elapsed timer under the Huddle badge: '2m'. Style it dimly to avoid visual noise. WHY: Meetings in real offices have duration anxiety. A tiny '14m' counter under the huddle badge is funny (oh god they've been 'meeting' for 14 minutes and nobody is even working) and adds life to an otherwise static element.

ideas.title: Activity Log Entry Animations
ideas.description: In the dashboard tab, ActivityLog entries appear instantly when new data arrives. There's no transition. The worker cards have enter/exit animations (worker-card--new, worker-card--fading) but the activity log has none. Fix: add a CSS animation to .activity-entry: @keyframes entry-slide { from { opacity: 0; transform: translateY(-8px); } to { opacity: 1; transform: translateY(0); } } .activity-entry { animation: entry-slide 0.3s ease both; }. Use animation-delay based on index for stagger: style={{ animationDelay: `${i * 0.05}s` }}. WHY: The activity log is the narrative spine of the dashboard. Static entries feel like a database dump. Staggered slide-in makes each event feel like it's 'arriving' — matching the real-time nature of the data.

ideas.title: Bloberto's Mood Board — ASCII Art Desk Doodle
ideas.description: Add a tiny 'doodle' area below the whiteboard (or as a hover reveal on the whiteboard). It shows a 3-line ASCII art that changes with vibe. crushing: '╰(*°▽°*)╯', on-fire: '(╯°□°)╯︵ ┻━┻', in-flow: '(⌐■_■)', slow-day: '(._. )', after-hours: '(∪.∪ )...zzz'. Render it as a <pre> element inside the whiteboard div, below the history dots, with font-size: 0.3rem and opacity: 0.3. On hover, opacity jumps to 0.7. WHY: This is pure personality. It costs 5 lines of JSX and tells the user 'someone with taste made this.' Doodles on a whiteboard are universally human — they bridge the gap between 'data dashboard' and 'someone's actual office.' The hover reveal makes it feel like a discovered secret, not a decoration.
